prompt:
You are an OCR system. TRANSCRIBE this typewritten document exactly.

Read every word from top to bottom, left to right.
Copy ALL text - titles, paragraphs, lists, signatures, footnotes.

Preserve formatting:
- Keep line breaks where they appear
- Maintain paragraph spacing
- Preserve indentation

Output ONLY the transcribed text.

smolvlm-500m.notes: Larger SmolVLM, better OCR benchmark scores

smolvlm2-500m.notes: SmolVLM2 series, video-focused but OCR capable

smoldocling-256m.notes: Document-specialized, trained for OCR with DocTags

details.actual:
The clock on the mantle she finally sat at.

The transcription of the document shows that the text is a letter from the writer to someone named Sarah. The letter is dated March 18, 1996. The writer expresses regret for not being able to attend the event, but acknowledges that she will be there. The writer also mentions that she will be leaving the event to attend a different one.
details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual: The morning train.
details.expected:
THE MORNING TRAIN

	The platform empty, still, and grey,
	Before the crowd arrives --
	I stand alone and watch the rails
	Stretch out like silver knives.

	A distant whistle, low and long,
	Cuts through the morning air.
	The engine rounds the final bend,
	All steam and smoke and glare.

	The doors slide open, people rush,
	A hundred lives collide.
	And I step on, and find my seat,
	And watch the world outside.

		* * *

	The city shrinks behind the glass,
	The fields roll into view.
	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

details.actual: A Brief History of Photography.
details.expected:
A BRIEF HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY
Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams

I.   Early Experiments (1820-1840)
     A. Camera obscura principles
     B. Niepce and the first photograph
     C. Daguerre's contributions
	  1. The daguerreotype process
	  2. Commercial applications

II.  The Wet Plate Era (1851-1880)
     A. Collodion process
     B. Advantages over daguerreotype
	  - Shorter exposure times
	  - Reproducible negatives
     C. Notable practitioners
	  1. Mathew Brady
	  2. Julia Margaret Cameron

III. The Kodak Revolution (1888-1920)
     A. "You press the button, we do the rest"
     B. Roll film development
     C. Amateur photography emerges

IV.  Questions for Discussion
     - How did photography change portraiture?
     - What social changes did it enable?

details.actual: Reading the text, I can see that the document is a planning committee meeting.
details.expected:
PLANNING COMMITTEE MEETING
November 3, 1958
----------------------------

Present: J. Harrison, M. Webb, R. Collins, S. Park

1. Budget Review
   - Current balance: $4,250.00
   - Outstanding invoices: $890.00
   - Projected surplus: $1,100.00

2. Spring Festival Planning
   a) Date confirmed: May 15-17
   b) Venue: Memorial Park (pending approval)
   c) Entertainment committee to report by Dec. 1

3. Action Items
   Harrison  --  Contact city office re: permits
   Webb      --  Draft volunteer schedule
   Collins   --  Get quotes from three caterers
   Park      --  Reserve tables and chairs

Next meeting: November 17, 2:00 PM

details.actual:
October 15, 1962
Dear Margaret,
I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversation often.

The autumn has been particularly beautiful
this year. The mages along the drive have turned
brilliant shades of orange, and the garden is
still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.
I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned
the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been
unable to find you at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me your name.

With warm regards,
P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.
details.expected:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

	I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversations often.

	The autumn here has been particularly beautiful
this year. The maples along the drive have turned
a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is
still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

	I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned --
the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been
unable to find it at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me the author's name.

		With warm regards,
		Eleanor

P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.

details.actual: [Page 12] March 12
details.expected:
March 12

	Woke early today -- before six -- and could not
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun
come up over the rooftops.

	The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink.
Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes
to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what
can you do?

	Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the
Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

	"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

details.actual: How to Develop Black and White Film
details.expected:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop

	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual:
The morning train
The first form empty, still, and grey,
Before the crowd arrives --
I stand alone and watch the rails
Stretch out like silver knives.
A distant whistle, low and long,
Cuts through the morning air.
The engine rounds the final bend,
All steam and smoke and glare.
The door slide open, people rush
A hundred lives collide.
And I stare on, and find my seat,
And watch the world outside.
** * *  The city shrieks behind the glass,
The field rolls into view.
And somewhere in this rocking car,
I start my life anew.
---  Anonymous, 1947
details.expected:
THE MORNING TRAIN

	The platform empty, still, and grey,
	Before the crowd arrives --
	I stand alone and watch the rails
	Stretch out like silver knives.

	A distant whistle, low and long,
	Cuts through the morning air.
	The engine rounds the final bend,
	All steam and smoke and glare.

	The doors slide open, people rush,
	A hundred lives collide.
	And I step on, and find my seat,
	And watch the world outside.

		* * *

	The city shrinks behind the glass,
	The fields roll into view.
	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

details.actual:
A brief history of photography. lecture outline - Prof. Williams.

I. Early experiments (1820-1840)
A. Camera obscura principles
B. Niepce and the first photograph
C. Daguerre's contributions
1. The daguerreotype process
2. Commercial applications

II. The wet plate era (1851-1880)
A. Collodion process
B. BB advantages over daguerreotype
- Shorter exposure times
- Reproducible negatives
C. Notable practitioners
1. Mathew Brady
2. Julia Margaret Cameron

III. The Kodak revolution (1888-1920)
A. "You press the button, we do the rest"
B. Roll film development
C. Amateur photography emerges

IV. Questions for discussion
- How did photography change portraiture?
- What social changes did it enable?

details.actual:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

I hope this letter finds you well. It has been far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself thinking of our conversations often. The autumn has been particularly beautiful this year. The maples along the drive have turned a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned - the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been unable to find it at our local shop, and I wondered if you might be the author's name.

With warm regards,

Blair
details.expected:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

	I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversations often.

	The autumn here has been particularly beautiful
this year. The maples along the drive have turned
a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is
still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

	I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned --
the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been
unable to find it at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me the author's name.

		With warm regards,
		Eleanor

P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.

details.actual:
March 12 
Woke early today -- before six -- end could not 
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun 
come up over the rooftops. 
The lumber 3 weeks of finally came about the kitchen sink. 
Three weeks of fixing it and took twenty minutes 
to fix. The bill it comes about $45.00. Highway robbery, but what exemyx 
can you do? 
Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the 
hemingway and picked up one more. 
"The Old Man and the Sea" 
"Beast of Eden" by Steinbeck 
Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far. 
- Call Mother on Sunday 
-Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit) 
- Pay electric bill before the 20th 
To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.
details.expected:
March 12

	Woke early today -- before six -- and could not
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun
come up over the rooftops.

	The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink.
Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes
to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what
can you do?

	Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the
Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

	"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

details.actual:
How to Develop Black and White Film Materials Needed:

- Develop a Black and White Film
- Developer (D-76 recommended)
- Stop Bath (50-70 degrees F)
- Fixer (65-70 degrees F)
- Developer into Tank
- Start Timer
- Agitate for 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
- Total time: 7-9 minutes
- Film Data Sheet
- Stop and Fix
- Pour out Developer for 5 minutes
- Add Stop Bath for 30 sec.
- Hang to dry in dust-free area
- Wash and dry
- Caution: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.
details.expected:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams

     A. Camera obscura principles
     B. Niepce and the first photograph

details.expected:
March 12

	Woke early today -- before six -- and could not
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun
come up over the rooftops.

	The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink.
Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes
to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what
can you do?

	Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the
Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

	"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

details.expected:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.
